title: A Complete Unknown
logline: The story of Bob Dylan's rise from unknown folk singer to controversial electric performer, chronicling his journey from arriving in New York in 1961 to his polarizing performance at Newport Folk Festival in 1965.
antagonistOrConflict: The folk music establishment's resistance to artistic evolution and Bob's internal struggle between authenticity and artistic growth
tone: Intimate, reflective, with growing tension between tradition and innovation
themes: The artist's relationship with their audience

actBreaks.description: End of Act I - Bob establishes himself in the folk scene, gets his record deal, and begins his rise to fame

validation.ruling: Scene #38 at 26.4% closes Act 1 with Sylvie leaving for Italy, Bob's relationship with traditional structures breaking down, and his obsession with transcending musical categories. The Johnny Cash TV appearance reinforces themes of artistic evolution beyond labels, perfectly transitioning from Bob's folk apprenticeship to his period of creative independence.

actBreaks.description: End of Act II - Bob records 'Like a Rolling Stone' with electric instruments, marking his complete transformation

validation.ruling: Scene #89 at 64.8% ends Act 2 with Bob fully embracing electric music in the studio, using unconventional instruments (toy siren) and performing for large mainstream audiences with Joan. This represents his complete artistic transformation before the final confrontation with the folk establishment at Newport.

actBreaks.description: End of Act III - Bob's journey concludes at Greystone Hospital, having fully transformed from folk purist to electric innovator

validation.ruling: Scene #124 at 99.2% provides the perfect denouement - Bob returns to visit dying Woody Guthrie, bringing his journey full circle. The quiet moment with harmonica and Woody's music playing represents Bob's reconciliation with his folk roots after his electric revolution, showing growth and continuity rather than complete rejection.

naturalStructure.structuralPersonality: A classic three-act biographical rise-and-transformation story with strong character development arc
naturalStructure.pacingNotes: Deliberate build in Act I establishing the folk world, accelerating momentum in Act II as Bob gains fame and begins changing, intense dramatic confrontation in Act III culminating at Newport 1965

incitingIncident.description: Bob meets his idol Woody Guthrie for the first time, with Pete Seeger facilitating the introduction and allowing Bob to play a song for the legendary folk musician.
incitingIncident.sceneHeading: INT. WOODY’S ROOM -- GREYSTONE HOSPITAL -- CONTINUOUS

validation.ruling: Scene #10 perfectly captures the inciting incident where Bob meets both Pete Seeger and his idol Woody Guthrie for the first time. This pivotal encounter at 5.6% establishes Bob's entry into the folk music world and sets his entire journey in motion. The scene introduces key relationships and Bob's transformation from anonymous Minnesota kid to 'Bobby Dylan.'

midpoint.description: Six months later at the Kettle of Fish cafe, Bob sits transformed with frizzy hair, dark glasses, and a notepad, largely ignoring the table conversation that seems designed to get his attention. Folk musicians discuss his changing direction.
midpoint.sceneHeading: INT. THE KETTLE OF FISH -- SIX MONTHS LATER -- NIGHT

validation.ruling: Scene #73 at 51.2% serves as a clear midpoint where Bob has transformed from eager folk student to detached observer of the scene he once desperately wanted to join. The debate about whether traditional folk songs are 'fried' and 'over-played' directly reflects Bob's own artistic evolution. His physical separation (dark glasses, notepad, ignoring conversation) shows he's moved beyond this world while still being defined by it.

climax.description: Pete Seeger performs with six actual prisoners and an armed guard on stage. Alan Lomax approaches Pete backstage, saying they got nothing from Bob and that Pete must be the one to handle the situation.
climax.sceneHeading: EXT. NEWPORT FOLK FESTIVAL -- STAGE -- NIGHT

validation.ruling: Scene #111 at 82.4% represents the final setup before Bob's electric performance - Pete on stage with the prison work gang while Lomax desperately seeks someone to stop Bob. This is the last moment of the old folk world before Bob's revolutionary act. While brief, it captures the institutional panic and Pete's position as the establishment's last hope to prevent the change.

scenes.heading: INT. STATION WAGON -- WET DAY -- WINTER 1961
scenes.summary: Bob arrives in New York City via station wagon in winter 1961, carrying his guitar and a notebook with 'Song for Woody' written inside, dropped off at the Lincoln Tunnel entrance.

scenes.heading: EXT. 10TH AVENUE -- WET DAY -- MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Bob stands alone on 10th Avenue in Hell's Kitchen, consulting a newspaper clipping from his notebook before heading south into the city.

scenes.heading: INT. FEDERAL COURTROOM -- SOUTHERN DISTRICT -- WET DAY
scenes.summary: Pete Seeger appears before a federal judge for sentencing, defending his patriotism while facing charges related to his associations and the people he sang for.

scenes.heading: EXT. BLEEKER AND MACDOUGAL -- SAME WET AFTERNOON
scenes.summary: Bob explores the vibrant folk scene on MacDougal Street, encountering crowds of bohemians, beatniks, and folk music fans outside venues like Cafe Wha and The Gaslight.

scenes.heading: EXT. 110 MACDOUGAL STREET -- SAME
scenes.summary: Bob discovers the Folk-Lore Center record shop displaying works by Leadbelly, Woody Guthrie, and Pete Seeger, but finds it closed with a sign about being at the courthouse.

scenes.heading: INT. THE KETTLE OF FISH BAR -- SAME
scenes.summary: In the Kettle of Fish bar, Bob asks for directions to Greystone Hospital where Woody Guthrie is staying, meeting Dave Van Ronk who warns him it's a mental hospital.

scenes.heading: EXT. FEDERAL COURTHOUSE -- LATE WET AFTERNOON
scenes.summary: Pete Seeger performs 'This Land Was Made for You and Me' on the courthouse steps after his sentencing, surrounded by supporters and protesters.

scenes.heading: EXT. GREYSTONE HOSPITAL -- NEW JERSEY -- WET NIGHT
scenes.summary: Bob arrives at the gothic Greystone Hospital in New Jersey by taxi, spending his last two dollars to get there as night falls.

scenes.heading: INT. HALLWAY -- GREYSTONE HOSPITAL -- SAME
scenes.summary: Bob climbs the hospital stairs and discovers Pete Seeger playing banjo softly for the ailing Woody Guthrie in his room.

scenes.heading: INT. WOODY’S ROOM -- GREYSTONE HOSPITAL -- CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Bob meets his idol Woody Guthrie for the first time, with Pete Seeger facilitating the introduction and allowing Bob to play a song for the legendary folk musician.
scenes.turningPointNote: Bob's pilgrimage to meet Woody represents his entry into the folk tradition

scenes.heading: INT. / EXT. PETE SEEGER’S STATION WAGON -- SAME WET NIGHT
scenes.summary: Pete drives Bob through the night, discussing music tastes as Bob reveals his appreciation for everything from Little Richard to Johnny Cash, showing his broad musical interests.

scenes.heading: INT. / EXT. WASHINGTON HEIGHTS -- SAME
scenes.summary: Pete offers Bob a place to stay when he realizes the young musician has nowhere to go, despite Bob's claim of having friends downtown.

scenes.heading: EXT. SEEGER CABIN -- HUDSON VALLEY -- SAME WET NIGHT
scenes.summary: Bob arrives at Pete's rustic cabin home in the Hudson Valley, meeting Toshi and marveling at their self-built home that looks 'like the picture on the maple syrup bottle.'

scenes.heading: INT. PETE SEEGER’S HOUSE -- SAME NIGHT
scenes.summary: Bob settles in at the Seeger house, forced to smoke outside due to Toshi's rules, while Pete discusses his legal troubles and potential tour plans with his wife.

scenes.heading: INT. SEEGER HOUSE -- PETE & TOSHI’S BEDROOM -- LATER
scenes.summary: Bob wakes early to write and play music by the Hudson River, working on songs including 'Girl from the North Country' while the Seeger children watch.

scenes.heading: INT. SEEGER HOUSE -- LIVING ROOM -- MORNING
scenes.summary: A month later, Bob watches from the wings as Pete Seeger performs at Town Hall, leading the audience in an African-inspired call-and-response song.

scenes.heading: INT. TOWN HALL CONCERT HALL -- A MONTH LATER
scenes.summary: Joan Baez arrives at Gerdes Folk City for her performance, greeted by eager fans outside the venue as anticipation builds for her show.

scenes.heading: EXT. MERCER STREET -- EARLY EVENING
scenes.summary: In her dressing room, Joan meets with manager Albert Grossman who reads her a glowing New York Times review before her performance.

scenes.heading: INT. THE GERDES FOLK CITY -- DRESSING ROOM -- SAME
scenes.summary: Joan Baez performs 'House of the Rising Sun' at Gerdes Folk City, captivating the packed audience including Pete Seeger and Bob, who locks eyes with her during the performance.
scenes.turningPointNote: Bob's first encounter with Joan establishes a crucial relationship

scenes.heading: INT. GERDES FOLK CITY -- LATER
scenes.summary: Bob busks in Washington Square Park with a partner, performing Woody Guthrie's children's songs and collecting donations before rushing off to catch a cab.

scenes.heading: EXT. WEST VILLAGE PARK -- DAY
scenes.summary: Bob arrives late for his Columbia Records session, met by an agitated Albert Grossman who is visibly armed and berates him for being over an hour late.

scenes.heading: EXT. COLUMBIA RECORDS -- MIDTOWN -- DAY
scenes.summary: In the elevator to Columbia Records, Albert reads Bob's positive New York Times review aloud, praising his talent despite his disheveled appearance.

scenes.heading: INT. COLUMBIA RECORDS BUILDING -- ELEVATOR -- SAME
scenes.summary: Bob records 'Fixin to Die' at Columbia Records while John Hammond and Albert Grossman observe from the control room, with Hammond giving direction about staying close to the microphone.
scenes.turningPointNote: Bob's first recording session marks his entry into the commercial music world

scenes.heading: INT. RECORDING STUDIO -- DAY
scenes.summary: Bob performs at a church blues jam, playing an original song with passionate intensity while being recorded by a man with a Nagra recorder assisted by a young woman named Gena.

scenes.heading: INT. RIVERSIDE CHURCH -- SAME
scenes.summary: After the performance, Bob walks with Sylvie, sharing stories about carnival freaks and theorizing that all compelling performers must be 'freaks' in some way.

scenes.heading: EXT. RIVERSIDE CHURCH -- SAME
scenes.summary: Bob and Sylvie watch 'Now, Voyager' at a movie theater, seeing Bette Davis and Paul Henreid in the classic romantic scene with cigarettes.

scenes.heading: INT. SYLVIE AND BOB IN THE MOVIE THEATER -- SAME
scenes.summary: Over dinner in Chinatown, Bob and Sylvie discuss the film's themes of self-transformation, with Bob arguing that people don't 'find themselves' but make themselves into something different.

scenes.heading: INT. CHINATOWN RESTAURANT -- LATER THAT NIGHT
scenes.summary: Walking through the Village at night, Sylvie explains her involvement with CORE and the Freedom Rides, as they part ways at a subway entrance with romantic tension building.

scenes.heading: EXT. BOB AND SYLVIE WALK THROUGH THE VILLAGE -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: Bob visits a record shop and sees multiple unsold copies of his own album while Joan Baez's records are completely sold out, highlighting the disparity in their success.

scenes.heading: INT. WEST VILLAGE RECORD SHOP -- NEW DAY
scenes.summary: Inspired by news coverage of KKK attacks on Freedom Riders buses, Bob begins writing 'Blowin' in the Wind' on a napkin in a Village bar.
scenes.turningPointNote: Creation of Bob's most famous protest song

scenes.heading: INT. WEST VILLAGE BAR -- SAME
scenes.summary: Bob plays his newly written 'Blowin' in the Wind' for Woody Guthrie at the hospital, but is interrupted by an orderly who insists on quiet time.

scenes.heading: INT. GREYSTONE HOSPITAL -- WOODY’S ROOM -- DAY
scenes.summary: Bob and Sylvie attend a civil rights rally where a speaker discusses the lack of conscience in America regarding racial injustice and the need for more direct action.

scenes.heading: EXT. BOB AND SYLVIE AT A CIVIL RIGHTS RALLY -- DAY
scenes.summary: A mailman delivers a package addressed to 'Zimmerman' to Bob, revealing his real name to Sylvie as they prepare for a party at his apartment.

scenes.heading: EXT. BOB’S APARTMENT -- AFTERNOON -- SAME
scenes.summary: At Bob's apartment party, folk musicians debate musical categories while Sylvie's sister Gena discovers Bob's notebook filled with songs and writings.

scenes.heading: INT. BOB’S APARTMENT -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: After the party ends, Sylvie lies in bed watching Bob obsessively work on his music, playing along with Leadbelly records late into the night.

scenes.heading: INT. BOB'S APARTMENT -- NIGHT -- LATE
scenes.summary: At dawn, Bob continues writing feverishly, working on 'A Hard Rain's A-Gonna Fall' while Sylvie urges him to sleep, showing his driven creative process.

scenes.heading: INT. BOB’S APARTMENT -- DAWN -- NEXT MORNING
scenes.summary: By late summer, Sylvie packs for a trip to Italy while Bob watches Johnny Cash on TV, discussing the artificial nature of musical categories and labels.

scenes.heading: INT. BOB’S APARTMENT -- DAY -- LATE SUMMER
scenes.summary: Pete Seeger arrives at a small Southern church with Alan Lomax and an organizer, encountering state police and white protesters opposing integration efforts.

scenes.heading: EXT. A SMALL SOUTHERN CHURCH -- DAY
scenes.summary: Inside the Albany, Georgia church, Pete witnesses powerful spiritual singing and community solidarity among civil rights activists, deeply moved by the experience.
scenes.turningPointNote: Pete's direct involvement in civil rights demonstrates the folk movement's political engagement

scenes.heading: INT. ALBANY, GEORGIA -- SMALL SOUTHERN CHURCH -- DAY
scenes.summary: Joan gives a careful interview to a reporter about her music and approach to traditional songs, interrupted by young fans seeking autographs.

scenes.heading: INT. VILLAGE COFFEE HOUSE -- DAY
scenes.summary: Bob watches President Kennedy's address about the Cuban Missile Crisis from his apartment, noticing all his neighbors are also tuned in to the historic moment.

scenes.heading: INT. BOB’S APARTMENT -- NIGHT -- SAME
scenes.summary: Pete and Toshi return home from their civil rights trip to find their babysitter fleeing and their children worried about the possibility of war starting.

scenes.heading: EXT. SEEGER HOUSE -- NIGHT -- SAME
scenes.summary: The Seeger family watches the Cuban Missile Crisis coverage together, with Pete expressing fear that Kennedy's actions could lead to nuclear war.

scenes.heading: INT. SEEGER HOUSE -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: Joan sits alone in her Chelsea Hotel room during the crisis, unable to reach anyone by phone while watching Cronkite's dire news coverage about nuclear preparation.

scenes.heading: INT. CHELSEA HOTEL ROOM -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: Bob enters a nearly empty diner where staff and customers are glued to crisis coverage, the cook too distracted to serve as people prepare for potential nuclear war.

scenes.heading: INT./EXT. DINER -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: Joan walks through eerily empty Village streets looking for transportation as families flee the city, hearing Bob's amplified voice coming from a nearby club.

scenes.heading: EXT. VILLAGE STREET -- NIGHT -- SAME
scenes.summary: Joan finds Bob performing 'Masters of War' at The Gaslight for a crowd of people with nowhere else to go during the nuclear crisis, providing comfort through music.
scenes.turningPointNote: Bob and Joan come together during the crisis, establishing their relationship

scenes.heading: INT. THE GASLIGHT -- NIGHT -- SAME
scenes.summary: Bob and Joan wake together after the crisis passes, learning the world has stepped back from nuclear destruction as normal life resumes outside.

scenes.heading: INT. BOB’S APARTMENT -- DAWN
scenes.summary: In the aftermath, Joan questions Bob about his background and musical training, but he remains evasive about his past, claiming to have learned at carnivals.

scenes.heading: EXT. SEEGER CABIN -- MORNING
scenes.summary: Pete Seeger sits peacefully at his cabin, playing 'Little Boxes' quietly as his family enjoys breakfast inside, finding solace in music after the week's tensions.

scenes.heading: INT/EXT. BOB’S APARTMENT / HALLWAY -- DAY
scenes.summary: Bob frantically cleans his apartment as Sylvie arrives with luggage from a taxi three stories below. He rushes to meet her in the hallway with flowers, finding a hair clip that suggests another woman's presence.

scenes.heading: INT. COLUMBIA RECORDS STUDIO “A” -- DAY
scenes.summary: In Columbia Records Studio A, Bob performs for new producer Tom Wilson and Albert Grossman. Wilson is impressed when told Bob wrote the song himself.

scenes.heading: EXT. JONES & 4TH STREET -- SNOW -- DAY
scenes.summary: Bob and Sylvie play in the snow while a photographer captures them with a Hasselblad camera. Bob is cold and Sylvie tries to get him to wear a sweater as he sings.

scenes.heading: EXT. SAN FRANCISCO CONCERT -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: Joan Baez performs Bob's song at an outdoor concert in San Francisco while a crew records her performance. The scene transitions from Bob's version to Joan's interpretation.

scenes.heading: INT. BOB’S APARTMENT -- DAY
scenes.summary: Albert supervises a photo crew in Bob's apartment while Bob hides in the bedroom with Sylvie, wrapping a turntable as a gift for Woody. Bob is uncomfortable with the intrusion and prepares to leave for the airport.

scenes.heading: EXT. HIGHWAY 1 -- NORTHERN CALIFORNIA
scenes.summary: Bob rides his motorcycle up the Northern California coast highway to Joan Baez's house in Carmel Highlands, where she greets him at the door with a smile.

scenes.heading: INT. JOAN’S HOUSE -- CARMEL HIGHLANDS, CALIFORNIA
scenes.summary: Inside Joan's beautiful house, Bob admires the space while his new record plays. Joan shows him around and corrects his assessment of her guitar's condition when he claims it has buzz.

scenes.heading: EXT. MONTEREY FESTIVAL -- DAY
scenes.summary: At the Monterey Festival, Bob receives a lukewarm reception from the crowd of young people gathered on the rolling acres. The host introduces him as the songwriter behind other artists' hits, which makes both Bob and Joan wince.

scenes.heading: EXT. ONSTAGE AT MONTEREY -- MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Bob struggles with the tepid crowd response until Joan Baez joins him on stage with her guitar. She suggests he play a new song and the crowd cheers enthusiastically when she greets them.

scenes.heading: EXT. EL PASO -- CLUB PARKING LOT -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: In an El Paso club parking lot, a skinny man in black (Johnny Cash) breaks away from his group to listen to Bob Dylan singing on a car radio, climbing into strangers' El Camino to hear better.

scenes.heading: INT. LOBBY -- CBS / COLUMBIA RECORDS -- DAY
scenes.summary: Bob visits CBS/Columbia Records and is stunned to receive a large mail sack full of fan letters, plus two special envelopes including what appears to be a substantial check.

scenes.heading: EXT. 52ND STREET / CBS RECORDS -- DAY
scenes.summary: Walking on 52nd Street, Bob opens the letters - one contains a ten thousand dollar check from CBS, the other has an elaborate return address from Johnny Cash with multiple descriptive titles.

scenes.heading: EXT. PARK -- DAY
scenes.summary: Sitting on a park bench, Bob reads Johnny Cash's letter praising his Freewheelin' album as Cash's most prized possession. Bob is deeply moved by the recognition from his musical hero.

scenes.heading: INT. NEW YORK CITY -- TOWN HALL THEATER -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: At Bob's first major solo concert at Town Hall, he performs for an audience that has changed from folk fans to Bob Dylan fans. Meanwhile, fans buy up his records at a Village record shop where he watches incognito.

scenes.heading: INT. GREYSTONE HOSPITAL -- WOODY'S ROOM -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: Pete Seeger visits Woody Guthrie in the hospital, where Bob's gift of a portable record player and his album cover are visible. Pete discusses his own modest chart success and considers a world tour while Woody makes physical contact.

scenes.heading: EXT. DC HOTEL -- LATE DAY
scenes.summary: Bob emerges from a DC hotel to screaming fans who grab and kiss him. He fights his way to a waiting car, overwhelmed by the aggressive attention and demanding the driver leave immediately.

scenes.heading: INT. EASTERN AIRLINES SHUTTLE -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: On an airplane, Bob writes a letter to Johnny Cash about the difficulty of sudden fame while other passengers stare and comment on him. He describes feeling pulverized by public recognition.

scenes.heading: INT. NORTH CENTRAL JETLINER -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: Johnny Cash reads Bob's letter during turbulence and writes a reply on an air sickness bag, promising to say hello to someone in Duluth and mentioning the challenges of fame and touring.

scenes.heading: EXT. CAFE -- MACDOUGAL STREET -- WINTER -- DAY
scenes.summary: Bob sits incognito at a corner cafe table reading Johnny's reply on the air sickness bag, laughing at Cash's humorous observations about folk singers, until fans spot him and he must evacuate.

scenes.heading: INT. BOB’S APARTMENT -- DAY
scenes.summary: Bob and Sylvie watch Walter Cronkite announce President Kennedy's assassination on their small television. Both are ashen and riveted by the shocking news as Cronkite removes his glasses, overcome with emotion.
scenes.turningPointNote: Kennedy's assassination marks a cultural turning point that affects Bob's worldview and artistic direction

scenes.heading: EXT. NEWPORT FOLK FESTIVAL -- 1964 -- DAY
scenes.summary: At the 1964 Newport Folk Festival, Joan performs while the crowd chants for Bob. He watches from backstage, sees Sylvie in the audience, and observes Johnny Cash arriving unsteadily with his band while Pete manages the backstage area.

scenes.heading: EXT. NEWPORT FOLK FESTIVAL -- 1964 -- LATER
scenes.summary: Johnny Cash finishes his set praising Bob as his pen pal, describing Bob's mind as a blank paper with the best marks ever made. Bob takes the stage to enthusiastic applause while Johnny exits and Bob acknowledges the crowd.

scenes.heading: INT. THE KETTLE OF FISH -- SIX MONTHS LATER -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: Six months later at the Kettle of Fish cafe, Bob sits transformed with frizzy hair, dark glasses, and a notepad, largely ignoring the table conversation that seems designed to get his attention. Folk musicians discuss his changing direction.
scenes.turningPointNote: Bob's physical and artistic transformation signals his evolution away from traditional folk music

scenes.heading: EXT. MACDOUGAL STREET -- SAME
scenes.summary: Bob bursts out of the cafe onto MacDougal Street, which has changed with more money and tourists. He passes various musical venues with different genres playing as people stare, then approaches his Triumph motorcycle.

scenes.heading: EXT. BOB CRUISES ON THE TRIUMPH -- NIGHT -- SAME
scenes.summary: Bob rides his motorcycle through traffic without a helmet. When passengers in a car recognize him, he confirms his identity and guns the engine to jump the light and escape.

scenes.heading: INT. HAROLD LEVENTHAL’S APARTMENT -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: At Harold Leventhal's fundraising party for civil rights, Bob arrives with new girlfriend Becka. Albert Grossman observes from a corner while party guests debate whether songs can change the world, with pointed references to Bob's influence.

scenes.heading: INT. LEVENTHAL APARTMENT -- MINUTES LATER
scenes.summary: Becka watches as Bob tunes Pete's guitar surrounded by staring faces treating him like a curiosity. Pete gives a speech praising Bob's renewal power and longevity in folk music, while Bob stares at the floor respectfully.

scenes.heading: INT. LEVENTHAL APARTMENT -- LATER
scenes.summary: Albert escorts Bob and Becka through a gauntlet of people at the party's exit. Harold reminds Bob about upcoming benefits while Pete mentions a TV appearance, and Bob gets swarmed by well-wishers.

scenes.heading: INT. ELEVATOR -- SAME
scenes.summary: In the elevator, Bob complains to Becka that everyone wants him to be somebody else. Bobby Neuwirth, a disheveled drunk stranger, challenges Bob's statement, asking 'be what?' and introducing himself.

scenes.heading: EXT. WALKING ON THE STREET -- MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: On the street, Bob asks Neuwirth's name and destination, ignoring Becka's confusion. When Becka mentions Bob's tour with Joan Baez and asks if she's just more weight, Bob points at the building and says they want to own him.

scenes.heading: EXT. EAST VILLAGE STREETS -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: Bob finds McAnn's Irish bar in the East Village, hearing thumping bass music from inside where Neuwirth is performing.

scenes.heading: INT. MCANN’S -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: Inside McAnn's, Neuwirth plays raucous Celtic music with his band while Bob watches with fascination from a corner, devouring their camaraderie until a drunk woman recognizes him and chaos erupts as he tries to escape.

scenes.heading: INT. SYLVIE’S APARTMENT -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: At 4am, Bob bangs on Sylvie's door at her new apartment. She answers in a robe with another man sleeping in her bed. Bob sits numbly with a bloody, swollen lip as she tends to him, asking where his songs come from.

scenes.heading: INT. BOB’S APARTMENT -- MORNING
scenes.summary: In Bob's now-empty apartment with all signs of Sylvie gone, Bob lies in bed surrounded by song fragments and notebooks. He hears distant electric guitar music from the street and tunes his radio to find the source.

scenes.heading: INT. COLUMBIA STUDIO A -- DAY
scenes.summary: In Columbia Studio A, Bob sings 'Subterranean Homesick Blues' with a full band of session players while Tom Wilson, Albert Grossman, and John Hammond watch in shock from the control room, recognizing this will upset people.
scenes.turningPointNote: Bob's first full electric recording session represents his definitive break from folk tradition

scenes.heading: EXT. CHELSEA HOTEL -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: Neuwirth stumbles out of a taxi at Columbia Studios, retrieving both acoustic and electric guitar cases, including a Stratocaster with a British Airways sticker indicating Bob's new electric direction from his London trip.

scenes.heading: INT. HALLWAY -- CHELSEA HOTEL -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: In the Chelsea Hotel hallway, Bob searches for the right room until Joan opens her door with a joint, mentioning she saw Pete's TV show. Bob enters her room as she offers to share drugs.

scenes.heading: INT. CHELSEA HOTEL ROOM -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: In Joan's smoky hotel room, Bob frantically writes lyrics while Joan watches from bed. When she interrupts his creative process, he loses the lyric and they argue, leading to Joan kicking him out.

scenes.heading: INT. COLUMBIA STUDIO A -- DAY
scenes.summary: Back in Columbia Studio A, Bob records with a full session band including Neuwirth, using a toy siren and working on 'Highway 61 Revisited' while Tom Wilson and Albert observe the experimental electric sound.

scenes.heading: INT. LOMAX OFFICE -- WEST 46TH -- DAY
scenes.summary: At Alan Lomax's office, the Newport Folk Foundation board including Pete Seeger, Harold Leventhal, and others arrive via freight elevator to discuss the upcoming festival lineup and address concerns about electric music.

scenes.heading: INT. LOMAX OFFICE -- MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: The Newport board discusses performers including the Butterfield Blues Band, with tension arising over electric blues. They debate Bob's new electric direction while planning the festival, with clear resistance to the change from traditionalists.

scenes.heading: EXT. COLUMBIA STUDIO A -- JUNE 1965 -- DAY
scenes.summary: Neuwirth arrives at Columbia Studios by taxi, retrieving his guitar cases including a red Fender Stratocaster that Bob bought in London, signaling Bob's commitment to electric music.

scenes.heading: INT. COLUMBIA STUDIO A -- MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: In the empty studio, Tom Wilson watches Neuwirth set up Bob's new gleaming red Fender electric guitar, reacting with surprise when told Bob bought it in London while Neuwirth warms up the instrument.

scenes.heading: INT. COLUMBIA STUDIO A -- CONTROL BOOTH -- LATER
scenes.summary: (no summary)

scenes.heading: INT. STUDIO A -- JUST AS TAPE’S ABOUT TO ROLL
scenes.summary: (no summary)

scenes.heading: INT. COLUMBIA STUDIO A -- CONTROL BOOTH -- LATER
scenes.summary: (no summary)

scenes.heading: INT. PAUL SARGENT CLOTHING STORE -- DAY
scenes.summary: (no summary)

scenes.heading: EXT. PAUL SARGENT STORE -- 4TH STREET -- DAY
scenes.summary: (no summary)

scenes.heading: INT. SYLVIE’S APARTMENT -- MORNING
scenes.summary: (no summary)

scenes.heading: EXT. SYLVIE’S APARTMENT -- MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: (no summary)

scenes.heading: EXT. ON THE ROAD TO NEWPORT -- MORNING
scenes.summary: Bob and Sylvie ride his Triumph motorcycle toward Newport, leaving Manhattan behind on a summer morning. His song 'Mr. Tambourine Man' plays on car radios, with fans singing along in other vehicles.

scenes.heading: EXT. THE STREETS OF NEWPORT -- DAY
scenes.summary: Riding through Newport's main street, Bob is recognized by townspeople who point, nod, and call his name. He grins but keeps driving with Sylvie's hair flowing behind her.

scenes.heading: EXT. VIKING MOTEL -- NEWPORT -- DAY
scenes.summary: Bob and Sylvie arrive at the Viking Motel where musicians from around the world are gathered, creating a mix of international sounds. Jesse Moffette greets Bob and questions him about running with Sylvie.

scenes.heading: INT. BOB’S ROOM, THE VIKING MOTEL -- DAY
scenes.summary: In their motel room, Bob lights cigarettes for himself and Sylvie in a romantic gesture. Neuwirth bursts in with guitar cases - Bob's acoustic Gibson and the red Stratocaster from London.

scenes.heading: EXT. NEWPORT FOLK FESTIVAL -- DAY
scenes.summary: At the Newport Folk Festival, Joan performs on stage while Bob, Sylvie, and Neuwirth arrive backstage. Tension builds as Joan sees Bob with his guitar and discreetly flips him off, while Sylvie struggles with the situation.

scenes.heading: EXT. NEWPORT FOLK FESTIVAL -- CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Joan finishes her song and spontaneously starts singing Bob's 'Mama, You Been On My Mind' with flipped gender pronouns. Bob joins her for the second verse in a moment of musical connection.

scenes.heading: INT. DINER -- DOWNTOWN NEWPORT -- DAY
scenes.summary: Alan Lomax and Pete discuss Bob's intentions over lunch at a diner. Lomax expresses concern about Top 40 music being a 'shitstorm' and insists they must stand against it.

scenes.heading: INT. / EXT. VIKING HOTEL -- BOB’S SUITE -- LATE AFTERNOON
scenes.summary: Bob's motel suite is filled with smoke and party guests. Joan ghosts him while Neuwirth tells Bob that Sylvie left during his performance with Joan. Folk establishment leaders arrive unexpectedly.
scenes.turningPointNote: Personal and professional tensions converge as Bob loses Sylvie and faces confrontation from the folk establishment

scenes.heading: EXT. NEWPORT STREETS -- LATE DAY
scenes.summary: Bob speeds through Newport streets on his motorcycle, ignoring fans who wave and shout. He appears driven and agitated.

scenes.heading: EXT. PROVIDENCE FERRY TERMINAL -- LATE DAY
scenes.summary: At Providence Ferry Terminal, Bob catches up with departing Sylvie. They share cigarettes through a fence, but she tells him the 'carnival train' was fun but she's getting off, indicating their relationship's end.
scenes.turningPointNote: Bob loses his romantic anchor as Sylvie definitively leaves him

scenes.heading: EXT. NEWPORT FOLK FESTIVAL -- STAGE -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: Pete Seeger performs with six actual prisoners and an armed guard on stage. Alan Lomax approaches Pete backstage, saying they got nothing from Bob and that Pete must be the one to handle the situation.
scenes.turningPointNote: The folk establishment decides Pete must confront Bob directly

scenes.heading: EXT. NEWPORT -- VIKING PARKING LOT -- MORNING
scenes.summary: Pete sits in his pickup truck across from the Viking Motel, watching unhappily as Bloomfield and Al Kooper leave Bob's suite. Bob appears shirtless briefly before disappearing back inside.

scenes.heading: INT. BOB’S SUITE. VIKING MOTEL -- MORNING
scenes.summary: Pete enters Bob's trashed suite with coffee, finding bodies and debris everywhere. He discovers Bob in bed next to Albert Grossman, not Sylvie, and they discuss the folk establishment's repeated questioning.

scenes.heading: EXT. VIKING MOTEL -- MORNING
scenes.summary: Bob encounters Johnny Cash at the motel vending machine. Cash seems exhausted and high, initially not recognizing Bob. They discuss Cash staying for the finale and Bob's upcoming performance.

scenes.heading: INT. VIKING HOTEL, NEWPORT -- BOB’S SUITE -- LATE DAY
scenes.summary: Bob prepares in his suite, looking at himself in the bathroom mirror and briefly seeing a reflection from four years ago outside Guthrie's hospital room. Neuwirth reports it's getting chaotic outside.

scenes.heading: EXT. THE VIKING MOTEL -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: Bob and his band head to the festival for the electric performance. Backstage, all eyes are on Bob as he watches through his sunglasses. The entire folk establishment watches him with intense scrutiny before his controversial set.
scenes.turningPointNote: The climactic moment of Bob's electric performance at Newport, the central conflict of the film

scenes.heading: INT/EXT. CAR IN LOADING AREA -- SAME
scenes.summary: Bob gets in a car with Neuwirth and they leave as the festival finale plays in the background on stage.

scenes.heading: INT/EXT. CAR -- OVER BOB AND NEUWIRTH -- SAME
scenes.summary: Bob and Neuwirth drive to the Viking Motel where a party spills out onto the balcony, suggesting celebration or aftermath.

scenes.heading: INT. CROWDED ROOM AT THE VIKING -- LATER THAT NIGHT
scenes.summary: At the crowded Viking party, Bob sits apart watching the celebration. Albert reads him a positive Village Voice review calling him 'this generation's most awesome talent' despite the controversy.

scenes.heading: EXT. THE VIKING MOTEL -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: Pete and Toshi return to their motel room after the show. Pete sees the thumping party across the parking lot, spots Bob alone inside, but ultimately decides to walk away without confronting him.

scenes.heading: INT. PARTY ROOM AT THE VIKING -- DAWN
scenes.summary: At dawn, Bob sits alone in his chair as the party winds down around him. He gets up and heads for the door, leaving the celebration behind.

scenes.heading: EXT. THE VIKING HOTEL PARKING LOT -- DAWN
scenes.summary: In the parking lot at dawn, Joan tells Bob he won his freedom from 'all of us and our shit.' Bob responds that they didn't call her Judas. They share an emotional goodbye before Bob rides off on his Triumph.
scenes.turningPointNote: Bob and Joan's final reconciliation and farewell, acknowledging the cost of his artistic evolution

scenes.heading: EXT. NEWPORT FESTIVAL GROUNDS -- DAWN
scenes.summary: Bob rides along the festival grounds at dawn, finding Pete cleaning up trash. They exchange words through song lyrics, with Pete singing from Bob's 'Desolation Row,' revealing he did listen to Bob's album despite claiming otherwise.
scenes.turningPointNote: Pete and Bob's final reconciliation, with Pete acknowledging Bob's artistry

scenes.heading: INT. GREYSTONE HOSPITAL -- DAWN
scenes.summary: At Greystone Hospital at dawn, Bob sits with the ailing Woody Guthrie in his wheelchair. Bob plays harmonica along with a Woody Guthrie record as sunlight breaks through the trees, completing his journey back to his mentor.
scenes.turningPointNote: Bob returns to his roots and mentor, finding peace after his transformation
